tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst, z ktorego usunieto szesc zdan. Dobierz zdania oznaczone literami (A-H) tak, aby tekst byl spojny i logiczny. Dwa zdania zostaly podane dodatkowo i nie pasuja do zadnego fragmentu.
tasks.text:
THE HUMAN BRAIN

The human brain, weighing roughly 1.4 kilograms and containing approximately 86 billion neurons, is the most complex structure known to science. Each neuron can form thousands of connections with other neurons, producing a network whose total number of pathways exceeds the number of stars in the Milky Way. _________ (1.1) Despite extraordinary advances in neuroscience, we are still far from understanding how this three-pound organ generates consciousness, emotion, and thought.

Different regions of the brain are responsible for distinct functions. The cerebral cortex, the deeply folded outer layer, handles reasoning, language, and voluntary movement. Beneath it, the hippocampus is essential for forming new memories, while the amygdala processes emotional responses such as fear and pleasure. _________ (1.2) Damage to even a small area can produce dramatic changes in personality, memory, or perception.

One of the brain's most remarkable properties is neuroplasticity: its ability to reorganise itself by forming new neural connections throughout life. _________ (1.3) Although this capacity diminishes with age, it never disappears entirely, which is why stroke patients can relearn lost abilities through intensive rehabilitation.

Sleep plays a far more active role in brain function than scientists once believed. During sleep, the brain replays and consolidates the day's experiences, transferring information from short-term to long-term storage. _________ (1.4) Chronic sleep deprivation has been linked not only to impaired concentration and memory but also to an increased risk of neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's.

For much of history, reason and emotion were considered separate, even opposing forces. _________ (1.5) Patients with damage to the brain's emotional centres often struggle to make even simple decisions, demonstrating that feelings are not obstacles to rational thought but essential components of it.

Modern brain-imaging techniques such as functional MRI have revolutionised our understanding by allowing researchers to observe the brain in action. _________ (1.6) The ultimate goal of mapping every connection in the human brain, a project known as the connectome, remains one of the most ambitious undertakings in the history of science.

options.text: It is this staggering complexity that makes the brain both the most studied and the least understood of all human organs.
These regions do not operate in isolation but work together through intricate circuits, each contributing to the whole.
In children, the brain forms millions of new synaptic connections daily, sculpted by experience into the neural architecture that will serve them for life.
It also activates a waste-clearance system called the glymphatic pathway, which flushes out toxic proteins that accumulate during waking hours.
Modern neuroscience has overturned this view, revealing that the two are deeply intertwined and mutually dependent.
Yet these scans capture activity at a scale far coarser than individual neurons, meaning that much of what the brain does still occurs below the resolution of our instruments.
Artificial neural networks, inspired by the architecture of the biological brain, now power technologies ranging from language translation to image recognition.
The two hemispheres of the brain are connected by a thick bundle of nerve fibres called the corpus callosum, which allows them to share information.
